knot_proxy.verdict: NOT VALIDATED — knot-progress does not discriminate at the boundary; do not rank the second hump on it, use min_total and real solves instead

leak_note: Candidates were proposed by ranking on the stratified train slice, which overlaps the held-out aut-test rows, so the held-out fraction is an optimistic upper bound, not a clean transfer number.
knot_block_tie: The selection procedure chose a block climb (L - 2*max-block + 5*smaller-block) at 1000; it and the knot climb both reach 43/66 and both solve 7/7 held-out. The knot climb is recommended on principle; the block config is an equally-good empirical alternative.
harness_note: Runnable: experiments/heuristic_search/hsolve.py exposes greedy_search_h, a drop-in for greedy_baseline.greedy_search that takes the ordering as a config and returns the identical dict — certificate path, path_moves, min/max relators, every key — so nothing downstream changes. Verified three ways: config=None reproduces the baseline field for field; under a tuned ordering it pops identically to the research harness every number here was measured with; and its certificates replay independently through moves_to_states onto a trivial pair. Not yet exercised inside run_baseline's resume/W&B machinery, and it uses the string-keyed dict rather than the compact packed arena, so size memory accordingly at very large budgets.

distinct_problems.note: bins 4-7 are 24 rows but only 19 distinct automorphism classes

wave2.speedup_note: the three problems that resist cost the baseline only 13k-16k nodes - LESS than the two beaten 250x, so difficulty under length ordering does not predict difficulty under the knot ordering

knot_threshold.verdict: On hard presentations the knot term is the difference between solving three and solving none; on easy ones it is irrelevant. But the optimum does not climb with difficulty - it is a threshold to clear, not a dial to turn up, so one weight serves every stratum.

negatives.result: 1 of 216 arms beat its incumbent, on the weakest one, at one budget only - a null result
never beats two tiers at either budget, and costs two more parameters

unlocked no row the winner misses - but with no headroom to find one: the winner already solved every row of that experiment's own decidable set (a data-derived set over its own configs, not the fixed bins 4-7 band used elsewhere here), so no arm could have shown an improvement

unsolved124.note: Every one of the 124 unsolved AC-classes, each entered as its distinct signed-permutation relabels — 980 starts, not 992, because three of the classes are symmetric enough to be fixed by half the permutations — under all four best orderings, for 3,920 searches. Zero solves: the expected result, now measured over the whole target set rather than inferred from a six-row sample. These classes survived a 10^6-node search and 1,000 nodes is three orders of magnitude short.

scaling.note: Gap over the length baseline on the 24 decidable rows. Over the whole range every ordering widens (the baseline solves nothing below 500); what bears on a 10^6 run is the tail. Only the richer climb is still converting budget into new solves where the local ceiling cuts the curve off - and that agrees with EXP-06, which found the same crossover by a different route (richer 25->29 with budget while the lean winner plateaued 27->27).

threshold.note: What the endgame phase boundary is worth (budget 500 / 1000). It is load-bearing only for LEAN knot-only climbs: near the trivial state there is nothing structural left to buy, so an ordering carrying only a knot term wanders instead of cancelling and must be forced back to length. The richer climbs already carry smaller-block and max-knots, both of which fall as a pair approaches trivial, so they self-regulate. Consequence: the recommended ordering is a SINGLE weight vector with no phases at all.

portfolio.note: At a FIXED total budget, one deep search versus k shallow searches from k signed-permutation relabels. Depth wins monotonically for every ordering, with no crossover. The mechanism is that a divided search which succeeds almost always succeeds on the FIRST string it is given - the alternates run and contribute nothing at these depths, so splitting only shortens the search that was going to work.

union.note: The best single ordering solves 19 of the 24 decidable rows, but the union over every ordering tried in this program solves 23 - only ms596 (aut class 97) has resisted every configuration, across 607 recorded attempts. Different orderings solve different presentations, which is the same thing the 250x result says: the heuristic reorders difficulty rather than uniformly improving it.
